message:
feat(block,core,mvcc,repair): expand ARC cache hooks, MVCC concurrency, ext4 parsing, and repair evidence infrastructure

Multi-crate evolution across the FrankenFS stack:

- ffs-block: implement ARC cache memory pressure hooks for graceful degradation
  under memory contention (+60 lines of eviction policy and back-pressure logic)
- ffs-cli: add new CLI subcommands for repair and inspection workflows (+76 lines)
- ffs-core: expand core filesystem abstraction layer with new inode metadata,
  extent-tree traversal helpers, and error categorization (+149 lines)
- ffs-mvcc: extend MVCC layer with snapshot isolation improvements, conflict
  detection refinements, and transaction retry heuristics (+96 lines)
- ffs-ondisk: add ext4 extent-tree depth validation and overflow handling (+15 lines)
- ffs-repair: implement comprehensive repair evidence collection framework with
  structured forensic logging, damage assessment scoring, and recovery plan
  generation (+403 lines)
- beads: update issue tracking for active repair work

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2jk","title":"EPIC: Conformance & Quality Infrastructure","description":"# EPIC: Conformance & Quality Infrastructure

## PURPOSE
Establish the testing, benchmarking, golden-output, and E2E infrastructure that enables confident iteration on all other work.

This epic is foundational: without conformance fixtures, goldens, and reproducible E2E runs, we cannot prove correctness or detect regressions.

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md: "FrankenFS must include fixture-based conformance tests (goldens for ext4/btrfs metadata behavior), benchmark suite with baselines and regression detection, feature parity report with explicit percentages."

The current state has:
- `ffs-harness` with `ParityReport` and sparse fixtures
- Criterion benchmark scaffolding
- Some unit tests for core crates

What’s missing is the user-facing, reproducible contract:
- real ext4/btrfs images
- golden outputs with checksums
- CI gates
- E2E scripts with detailed logging and cleanup guarantees

## SCOPE (WHAT THIS EPIC MUST DELIVER)
1. Fixture images (ext4 + btrfs) with documented, reproducible contents.
2. Golden outputs for `ffs inspect` (at minimum), checksummed.
3. CI gate that fails on golden checksum mismatch.
4. Property-based tests for parsers (proptest) that validate invariants and “no panics on adversarial bytes”.
5. Performance baselines (hyperfine) + documented regression thresholds.
6. E2E smoke tests that exercise the user workflows and produce actionable logs + artifacts.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Golden output fixtures for ext4 inspect on 3+ real images
2. Golden output fixtures for btrfs inspect on 3+ real images
3. Hyperfine baseline measurements for all CLI commands
4. Property-based tests for parsing invariants (proptest)
5. CI gate that fails on golden checksum mismatch
6. sha256sum-based verification workflow documented

## DEPENDENCIES
None. This epic is the dependency root for most other work.

## RATIONALE
Per "Extreme Software Optimization": must have baselines before any optimization work.
Per "Porting to Rust": conformance harness is the arbiter, not vibes.
Per AGENTS.md: "No hand-wavy done claims without tests, metrics, and parity evidence."

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §7 (Conformance Harness)
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §8 (Benchmarking)

## Success Criteria
1. A new developer can run a single E2E command and get a PASS/FAIL with logs: `scripts/e2e/ffs_smoke.sh`.
2. CI fails on behavior changes unless goldens are explicitly updated via a scripted workflow.
3. Fixture generation is documented, repeatable, and produces identical goldens on the same toolchain.
4. Parser invariants are enforced by property tests and failures are easy to reproduce (seed + minimized case logged).
5. Baselines are recorded with environment metadata and regression thresholds are explicit.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-12T14:54:28.559990135Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:22.665571962Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","docs","harness"],"comments":[{"id":146,"issue_id":"bd-2jk","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

Mapped graveyard sections: 0.1 Optimization Loop + 0.7 Artifact Contract + 6.12 Proptest
EV score: 50 (Impact*Confidence*Reuse / (Effort*Friction))
Priority tier: A
Budgeted mode: bench_runs>=10, sample_floor=30 for noisy paths
Fallback trigger: golden-only gate, disable adaptive controller deployment

Required proof artifacts for execution on this bead:
- Baseline + hotspot evidence before changes.
- Isomorphism proof block per lever.
- Evidence-ledger event for decision path + fallback invocation.
- Repro pack: env.json, manifest.json, repro.lock (and LEGAL.md if IP risk appears).","created_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:22Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2jk.1","title":"Create ext4 golden fixture images","description":"# Create ext4 golden fixture images

## GOAL
Expand the ext4 "real image" corpus used by conformance + E2E, with a documented, reproducible generator workflow.

This repo already has:
- Sparse ext4 fixtures in `conformance/fixtures/*.json`.
- One kernel-derived golden reference JSON in `conformance/golden/ext4_8mb_reference.json` (validated by `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs`).

This bead extends that foundation with additional ext4 variants so read-path features (allocator/orphans/journal integration) have stronger regression coverage.

## CANONICAL PATHS (USE THESE EVERYWHERE)
- Sparse fixtures: `conformance/fixtures/*.json` + `conformance/fixtures/checksums.sha256`
- Kernel reference goldens: `conformance/golden/*.json` + `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`
- Generated binary images (not checked in by default): `conformance/golden/*.ext4` (see `conformance/golden/.gitignore`)

## DELIVERABLES
1. Add at least 2 additional ext4 kernel-reference goldens, each with:
   - A deterministic generation recipe (mkfs parameters + debugfs population commands).
   - A checked-in golden JSON file in `conformance/golden/` using the existing `ffs-harness::GoldenReference` schema.
   - A checksum entry in `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`.
2. Extend harness tests so these goldens are meaningful regression gates:
   - Either extend `crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs` or add a new ext4-variants test.
   - For each variant: generate the image at test time, parse it with `ffs_ondisk::Ext4ImageReader`, and compare against the golden JSON.
   - On mismatch, log field-level diffs (path/field/expected/actual) so failures are actionable.
3. Add a script to (re)generate all ext4 golden JSONs with detailed logging:
   - Preferred: extend `scripts/capture_ext4_reference.sh` to support variants.
   - Alternative: add `scripts/capture_ext4_references.sh` that produces all ext4 golden JSONs into `conformance/golden/`.
4. Ensure `scripts/verify_golden.sh --update` continues to work with the new JSON files.

## IMAGE VARIANTS (EXAMPLES, PICK 2+)
- A larger geometry image (e.g., 64MiB, 4KiB blocks) to vary group counts.
- `dir_index` enabled with a directory containing enough entries to force htree behavior.
- `sparse_super` enabled.
- A "nearly-full" image to stress free-block accounting.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Scripts print tool versions, timestamps, and every command executed.
- Tests print the exact mkfs/debugfs parameters used.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. New ext4 golden JSON files are checked in and covered by `conformance/golden/checksums.sha256`.
2. Harness tests compare generated images to those goldens and fail on drift.
3. `scripts/verify_golden.sh` passes in a clean tree.
4. A developer can intentionally regenerate goldens using the documented script(s).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T14:54:44.142980605Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T08:31:41.133475548Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T08:31:41.133407902Z","close_reason":"Added multiple ext4 golden variants, variant conformance tests, and capture script; all gates + verify_golden passed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","ext4","harness","ondisk"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2jk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T14:54:44.142980605Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":19,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.1","author":"ubuntu","text":"Reopened: Reopening during beads plan audit: these were closed without a recorded completion rationale, but remaining deliverables (fixture expansion/CI wiring) are still needed.","created_at":"2026-02-12T21:18:46Z"},{"id":22,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.1","author":"ubuntu","text":"Reopened: Deliverables not yet implemented: only 1 ext4 kernel reference golden exists in conformance/golden/.","created_at":"2026-02-12T21:47:08Z"},{"id":34,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"FOUNDATION: Golden fixture images are the single most important deliverable for regression testing. Every conformance, E2E, and parity test depends on having known-good images. Create ext4 images with: mkfs.ext4 with various block sizes (1K/2K/4K), feature flag combinations, nested directories, symlinks, hardlinks, xattrs, and files of varying sizes. Document image contents in a manifest.json.","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:33Z"},{"id":55,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progress update (AmberFalcon): implemented multi-variant ext4 golden pipeline. Added deterministic capture variants ext4_8mb_reference, ext4_64mb_reference, ext4_dir_index_reference in scripts/capture_ext4_reference.sh with timestamped command logging and tool-version logging. Added/updated harness regression coverage in crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs to generate and verify all variants with explicit variant/path/field mismatch context. Generated/updated conformance/golden/ext4_8mb_reference.json plus new ext4_64mb_reference.json and ext4_dir_index_reference.json, and refreshed conformance/golden/checksums.sha256. Validation: scripts/verify_golden.sh PASS; cargo fmt --check PASS; cargo check --all-targets PASS; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings PASS; cargo test --workspace PASS (using CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target-codex-gates).","created_at":"2026-02-13T08:30:23Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2jk.10","title":"Capture baseline p50/p95/p99 for read/write/fsync/mount","description":"# Capture baseline p50/p95/p99 for read/write/fsync/mount

## GOAL
Establish performance baselines for all critical filesystem operations so that future changes can be measured against a known reference point. Without baselines, we cannot detect performance regressions.

## OPERATIONS TO BENCHMARK

### Read Path
- Sequential read: 4KB blocks, 100MB file, measure IOPS and throughput
- Random read: 4KB blocks, uniform random offset, measure p50/p95/p99 latency
- Metadata read: stat() 10000 files, readdir() 1000 directories

### Write Path (once available)
- Sequential write: 4KB blocks, 100MB file
- Random write: 4KB blocks, uniform random offset
- Small file create: 10000 files, 4KB each
- mkdir: 1000 directories

### fsync
- fsync after single write
- fsync after 100 writes (batch)
- fdatasync vs fsync comparison

### Mount
- Cold mount: time from mount command to first FUSE response
- Warm mount: with existing cache/journal
- Recovery mount: after unclean shutdown

## OUTPUT FORMAT
- JSON report with all metrics
- Compare against previous baselines (stored in repo)
- CI integration: fail if p99 regresses > 20%

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Baselines captured for all listed operations
- [ ] Results stored as JSON artifacts in repo
- [ ] Benchmark harness is deterministic and reproducible
- [ ] CI gate: alerts on regression > 20% from baseline
- [ ] Documentation: how to run benchmarks locally","notes":"IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: Use criterion.rs for benchmark harness. Each benchmark should produce JSON output with schema: {operation, metric, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us, throughput_ops_sec, throughput_mb_sec}. Store baseline results in artifacts/baselines/perf_baseline.json. CI regression check compares current run against stored baseline. For mount latency, measure wall-clock time from process start to first successful FUSE stat() response. For read/write benchmarks, use 256MB test image with known file layout (1000 files, deterministic content). Random read benchmark must use uniform random with fixed seed for reproducibility.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:26:49.831178454Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:20.282458032Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["baseline","benchmark","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.10","depends_on_id":"bd-2jk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:26:49.831178454Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":131,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

Cluster: runtime_backpressure
Mapped graveyard sections: 0.5 Graceful Degradation + 10.3 Learning-Augmented Online + 12.13 Change-point
EV score: 16 (Impact*Confidence*Reuse / (Effort*Friction))
Priority tier: A
Budgeted mode: queue_budget=1024, throttle_sleep_ms<=25, shed_ratio_cap=0.15
Fallback trigger: deterministic threshold gate with Emergency=read-only

Required proof artifacts for execution on this bead:
- Baseline + hotspot evidence before changes.
- Isomorphism proof block per lever.
- Evidence-ledger event for decision path + fallback invocation.
- Repro pack: env.json, manifest.json, repro.lock (and LEGAL.md if IP risk appears).","created_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:20Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2jk.11","title":"Extend EvidenceLedger for repair and MVCC decisions","description":"# Extend EvidenceLedger for repair and MVCC decisions

## GOAL
Extend the existing EvidenceLedger infrastructure to cover both repair decisions (RaptorQ self-healing) and MVCC decisions (transaction abort, conflict resolution, version GC). Every significant system decision should be auditable.

## NEW EVENT TYPES

### MVCC Events
- TxnAborted { txn_id, reason: enum { FcwConflict, SsiCycle, Timeout, UserAbort } }
- VersionGC { block_id, versions_freed, oldest_retained_commit_seq }
- SnapshotAdvanced { old_commit_seq, new_commit_seq, versions_eligible }

### Write-Back Cache Events
- FlushBatch { blocks_flushed, bytes_written, flush_duration_us }
- BackpressureActivated { dirty_ratio, threshold }
- DirtyBlockDiscarded { block_id, txn_id, reason: Abort }

### Policy Events
- DurabilityPolicyChanged { old_overhead, new_overhead, posterior_params }
- RefreshPolicyChanged { block_group, old_policy, new_policy }

## INTEGRATION
- All components emit events through a shared EvidenceLedger trait
- EvidenceLedger implementation persists to JSONL (see bd-15c.8)
- In-memory buffer for high-frequency events (flush periodically)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All new event types defined and serializable
- [ ] MVCC components emit events for abort, GC, snapshot advance
- [ ] Write-back cache emits flush and backpressure events
- [ ] Events round-trip through JSONL serialization
- [ ] Unit tests for all new event types","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:03.920425781Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T04:02:10.892738289Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["evidence","ledger","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:25.657502673Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:19.648383025Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.11","depends_on_id":"bd-2jk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:03.920425781Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":89,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.11","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section. Every implementation bead must specify structured log events at trace/debug/info/warn/error levels with field names. Add logging spec before implementation begins.","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:04:19Z"},{"id":130,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.11","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

Mapped graveyard sections: 1.1 RaptorQ + 12.13 Change-point + 11.11 PoR
EV score: 25 (Impact*Confidence*Reuse / (Effort*Friction))
Priority tier: S/A

Fallback trigger: read-only degrade + evidence ledger + manual repair workflow

 {"id":"bd-2jk.12","title":"CI artifact gates for build and test outputs","description":"# CI artifact gates for build and test outputs

## GOAL
Implement CI gates that enforce artifact quality standards: binary size limits, test coverage thresholds, benchmark regression detection, and golden output verification.

## GATES

### Binary Size Gate
- Track release binary size per commit
- Fail if binary grows > 10% from baseline without explicit override
- Report size breakdown by crate (cargo bloat integration)

### Test Coverage Gate
- Minimum line coverage threshold: 60% (aspirational, increase over time)
- Per-crate coverage tracking
- Fail if coverage drops > 5% from previous commit

### Benchmark Regression Gate
- Run performance benchmarks on every PR
- Compare against stored baselines
- Fail if p99 latency regresses > 20%

### Golden Output Gate
- Conformance test outputs compared against golden checksums
- Fail if any golden output changes without explicit update

### Artifact Archive
- Store benchmark results, coverage reports, binary sizes as CI artifacts
- Historical tracking for trend analysis

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 4 gates implemented and running in CI
- [ ] Gates produce clear pass/fail with actionable error messages
- [ ] Override mechanism for intentional gate violations (explicit annotation)
- [ ] Historical artifacts accessible for trend analysis","notes":"IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE: Binary size gate should use cargo bloat for per-crate breakdown. Store baseline binary sizes in artifacts/baselines/binary_size.json. Test coverage gate should use cargo-llvm-cov with JSON output. Benchmark regression gate should integrate with criterion.rs baseline comparison. Golden output gate should compute BLAKE3 checksums of conformance test outputs and compare against artifacts/goldens/checksums.json. Override mechanism: add GATE_OVERRIDE=binary_size to PR description to bypass specific gates, with mandatory reviewer approval.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:14.622775001Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:26.751449820Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["artifacts","ci","gates"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2jk.12","depends_on_id":"bd-2jk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:14.622775001Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":170,"issue_id":"bd-2jk.12","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-2jk.13","title":"Decision Contract for repair policy (clean/minor/severe/io_stall states)","description":"# Decision Contract for repair policy (clean/minor/severe/io_stall states)

## GOAL
Define a formal decision contract for the self-healing repair policy. The contract specifies: states the system can be in, actions available in each state, loss function for each action, and calibration requirements.

## DECISION CONTRACT

### States S
- clean: no corruption detected, all checksums pass
- minor_corruption: < 1% blocks corrupted, repair symbols sufficient
- severe_corruption: 1-5% blocks corrupted, repair may be insufficient
- io_stall: I/O errors prevent reading some blocks (hardware degradation)

### Actions A
- repair_local: repair using local RaptorQ symbols (low cost, bounded capability)
- repair_global: full block group re-encode (higher cost, resets redundancy)
- defer_repair: schedule repair for next scrub cycle (risk of further degradation)
- degrade_readonly: switch to read-only mode (preserve data, lose writability)
- emergency_backup: trigger backup to external storage (highest cost, highest safety)

### Loss Function L(state, action)
- L(clean, repair_local) = overhead_only (unnecessary work)
- L(minor, repair_local) = small (expected case, low risk)
- L(minor, defer) = medium (corruption may spread)
- L(severe, repair_local) = medium (may not suffice)
- L(severe, defer) = high (data loss risk)
- L(io_stall, any_repair) = depends on I/O availability
- L(any, degrade_readonly) = writability_cost (acceptable for safety)

### Calibration
- Conformal coverage on corruption-risk estimates
- Posterior predictive check: does the Beta posterior accurately predict next-cycle corruption?
- If calibration fails: fall back to conservative policy (eager repair, higher overhead)

### Safety Envelope
- If corruption posterior crosses hard safety threshold (P > 0.05): force immediate repair
- If repair symbols insufficient: switch to read-only + alert
- Never silently continue with known unrecoverable corruption

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Decision contract formally specified in code (enum states, actions, loss)
- [ ] Policy engine selects optimal action given current state and loss function
- [ ] Conformal calibration check implemented
- [ ] Safety envelope enforced (hard thresholds)
- [ ] Evidence record for every policy decision

6. Generate ParityReport (bd-2jk.9) and verify no regressions
7. Exit 0 if all gates pass, exit 1 with detailed failure report

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Single command runs all conformance checks
2. Failure report identifies exact failing gate
3. Runs in CI in < 60 seconds
4.  {"id":"bd-32yn","title":"EPIC: Testing & Fault Injection","description":"# EPIC: Testing & Fault Injection

## PURPOSE
Build a comprehensive fault injection and stress testing framework for FrankenFS. A filesystem that cannot prove it handles failures correctly is not production-worthy. This epic covers: WAL interruption, partial repair, metadata corruption, I/O throttling, crash-replay testing, fsx-style stress, and xfstests compatibility.

## BACKGROUND
Kernel filesystems (ext4, btrfs, XFS) have decades of testing infrastructure: xfstests, fsx, blktests, syzkaller. FrankenFS must build equivalent coverage, with the added dimension of testing its unique features (MVCC, RaptorQ self-healing).

## SCOPE
1. Fault injection primitives (pluggable I/O layer with configurable failures)
2. WAL interruption tests (crash during journal write)
3. Partial repair tests (incomplete RaptorQ symbols)
4. Metadata corruption tests (corrupt specific metadata structures)
5. I/O throttling tests (slow I/O, deadline misses)
6. Crash-replay suite (deterministic crash schedules)
7. fsx-style stress (random operations + verification)
8. xfstests compatibility (run standard FS test suites)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Fault injection layer can inject failures at any I/O point
- [ ] All fault injection tests are deterministic and CI-executable
- [ ] Crash-replay covers 500+ random schedules
- [ ] fsx-style stress runs for configurable duration with corruption checking
- [ ] xfstests generic + ext4 subsets pass

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. Fault injection framework reusable across all scenarios
3. xfstests subset passes (bd-32yn.7)
4. Crash-replay suite covers 500+ schedules
5. CI-executable without hardware dependencies","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:46.527032547Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:19.952715237Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["faultinjection","reliability","testing"],"comments":[{"id":92,"issue_id":"bd-32yn","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW (lint fix): Epic is missing ## Success Criteria section.  {"id":"bd-32yn.1","title":"Fault injection: WAL interruption (crash mid-fsync)","description":"# Fault injection: WAL interruption (crash mid-fsync)

## GOAL
Test filesystem behavior when a crash occurs during WAL/journal write (mid-fsync). This is the most critical crash scenario — partial journal records must be handled correctly.

## SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Crash during WAL entry write (before fsync)
- Begin transaction, write WAL entries, crash before fsync
- Expected: on recovery, transaction is cleanly aborted (no partial data)

### Scenario 2: Crash during fsync (partial write to disk)
- WAL entry partially written (torn write)
- Expected: CRC32C detects torn entry, entry discarded, transaction aborted

### Scenario 3: Crash after fsync but before commit record
- WAL data entries durable, but commit record not written
- Expected: transaction aborted (no commit = no visibility)

### Scenario 4: Crash during commit record write
- Commit record partially written
- Expected: CRC32C of commit record fails, transaction aborted

### Scenario 5: Crash after commit record (before cache flush)
- Transaction committed in WAL, but dirty cache blocks not flushed
- Expected: WAL replay reconstructs committed state

## IMPLEMENTATION
- Pluggable I/O layer with injectable failure points
- Failure injection: fail_at_byte(offset) — crash after writing N bytes
- Deterministic: same seed produces same crash point
- Recovery verification: mount after crash, verify filesystem consistent

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 5 scenarios produce correct recovery behavior
- [ ] No data corruption from partial WAL writes
- [ ] No silent data loss
- [ ] Deterministic and CI-executable
- [ ] Evidence records logged for all recovery actions","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:59.368753493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:41:02.489924878Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T18:41:02.489856841Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crash","fault","wal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.1","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:32.516893629Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.1","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:59.368753493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-32yn.2","title":"Fault injection: partial repair symbols (incomplete RaptorQ)","description":"# Fault injection: partial repair symbols (incomplete RaptorQ)

## GOAL
Test filesystem behavior when RaptorQ repair symbols are partially available — some symbols corrupted, some missing, or insufficient symbols for full recovery.

## SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Some repair symbols corrupted
- Corrupt 20% of repair symbols in a block group
- Inject data corruption within RaptorQ decode capacity
- Expected: repair succeeds using remaining symbols

### Scenario 2: Repair symbols insufficient
- Corrupt enough repair symbols that decode fails
- Inject data corruption that now cannot be repaired
- Expected: graceful failure, evidence record logged, no silent data loss

### Scenario 3: Symbol metadata corrupted
- Corrupt the symbol header (checksums, group descriptors)
- Expected: symbols detected as invalid, repair falls back to re-encode or fails gracefully

### Scenario 4: Stale symbols (data changed after symbol generation)
- Write new data to blocks, do NOT refresh repair symbols
- Corrupt the new data blocks
- Expected: repair restores OLD data (stale), system detects version mismatch via BLAKE3

### Scenario 5: Symbol storage I/O errors
- Inject I/O errors when reading repair symbol blocks
- Expected: repair fails gracefully, evidence logged, no panic

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Partial symbol corruption: repair succeeds when capacity allows
- [ ] Insufficient symbols: graceful failure with evidence
- [ ] Stale symbols: version mismatch detected
- [ ] I/O errors: no panic, graceful degradation
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic and CI-executable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:11.847203864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T03:53:53.964225759Z","closed_at":"2026-02-16T03:53:53.964205691Z","close_reason":"Already implemented: tests for partial symbol corruption, insufficient symbols, stale symbols, metadata corruption, and symbol I/O errors exist in ffs-repair","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fault","raptorq","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.2","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:43.462398626Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.2","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:11.847203864Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":128,"issue_id":"bd-32yn.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

-{"id":"bd-32yn.3","title":"Fault injection: metadata corruption (superblock, GDT, inodes)","description":"# Fault injection: metadata corruption (superblock, GDT, inodes)

## GOAL
Test filesystem behavior when critical metadata structures are corrupted. Metadata corruption is catastrophic — it can make the entire filesystem unreadable.

## SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Superblock corruption
- Corrupt primary superblock fields (magic number, block count, inode count)
- Expected: mount fails with clear error, backup superblock used if available

### Scenario 2: Group Descriptor Table corruption
- Corrupt GDT entries (block bitmap location, inode bitmap, inode table)
- Expected: affected block groups inaccessible, other groups still readable

### Scenario 3: Inode corruption
- Corrupt individual inode fields (size, block count, extent tree root)
- Expected: affected file inaccessible, other files intact

### Scenario 4: Inode bitmap corruption
- Corrupt inode allocation bitmap
- Expected: allocation inconsistency detected, defensive read behavior

### Scenario 5: Block bitmap corruption
- Corrupt block allocation bitmap
- Expected: allocation inconsistency detected, read-only degradation

### Scenario 6: Extent tree internal node corruption
- Corrupt B-tree internal node in extent tree
- Expected: file partially readable (extents before corruption accessible)

### Recovery Testing
- For each scenario: verify RaptorQ repair (if metadata has 2x overhead) can recover
- If repair impossible: verify graceful degradation and clear error reporting

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Each metadata corruption type produces appropriate error response
- [ ] No panics from corrupted metadata
- [ ] Checksums detect corruption before use (no silent data misinterpretation)
- [ ] RaptorQ metadata repair works for recoverable cases
- [ ] Evidence ledger records all detected corruption
- [ ] All scenarios deterministic and CI-executable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:24.051971520Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:19.644548047Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","fault","metadata"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.3","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:24.252718351Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.3","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:24.051971520Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":127,"issue_id":"bd-32yn.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-32yn.4","title":"Fault injection: I/O throttling (slow I/O, deadline misses)","description":"# Fault injection: I/O throttling (slow I/O, deadline misses)

## GOAL
Test filesystem behavior under degraded I/O performance — simulating slow disks, network storage latency, or I/O congestion.

## SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Uniform slow I/O
- Add 50ms latency to every read/write operation
- Expected: filesystem remains functional, FUSE operations complete (slowly)
- Measure: FUSE operation timeout handling, queue depths

### Scenario 2: Intermittent I/O stalls
- Random 500ms-2s stalls on 5% of I/O operations
- Expected: no deadlocks, backpressure activates, operations eventually complete

### Scenario 3: I/O bandwidth limit
- Limit to 10MB/s read, 5MB/s write (simulating NAS or USB)
- Expected: FlushDaemon adapts, no OOM from dirty block accumulation

### Scenario 4: I/O timeout
- I/O operations that take > 30s return ETIMEDOUT
- Expected: operation fails gracefully, no resource leak

### Scenario 5: Progressive degradation
- Start with normal I/O, gradually increase latency over 60s
- Expected: backpressure system activates, evidence ledger records degradation

## IMPLEMENTATION
- Pluggable I/O layer with configurable delay, bandwidth limit, timeout
- Delay injection: uniform, gaussian, or bimodal distribution
- Integration: inject at ffs-block I/O boundary

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] No deadlocks under any I/O degradation scenario
- [ ] Backpressure correctly activates under I/O stalls
- [ ] No OOM from unbounded dirty block accumulation during slow I/O
- [ ] Operations fail gracefully on timeout (no resource leak)
- [ ] All scenarios CI-executable with deterministic delays","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:36.214496847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:26.362143810Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fault","io","throttle"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.4","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:36.214496847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":168,"issue_id":"bd-32yn.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-32yn.5","title":"Crash-replay test suite: 500 random schedules with injected crashes","description":"# Crash-replay test suite: 500 random schedules with injected crashes

## GOAL
Run 500 deterministic crash-replay scenarios: each generates a random sequence of filesystem operations, injects a crash at a random point, recovers, and verifies consistency.

## DESIGN

### Operation Generator
- Random sequence of: create, write, read, rename, unlink, mkdir, rmdir, fsync
- Operations parameterized: file size, directory depth, name length
- Deterministic: seeded PRNG produces reproducible sequences
- Length: 100-1000 operations per schedule

### Crash Point Selection
- For each schedule, select 1-3 random crash points
- Crash point = operation index + I/O sub-operation (e.g., crash during 3rd block write of operation #47)
- Ensures coverage of: mid-write, mid-fsync, mid-rename, mid-unlink crashes

### Recovery and Verification
After each crash:
1. Mount filesystem (triggers crash recovery)
2. Full filesystem walk: verify all accessible files are readable
3. Consistency check: no dangling inodes, no double-allocated blocks
4. Committed data check: all fsync'd data intact
5. Evidence ledger check: recovery actions logged

### Reporting
- Per-schedule: pass/fail, crash point, recovery time, evidence summary
- Aggregate: pass rate, mean recovery time, most common failure mode
- Any failure: save full reproducer (seed, schedule, crash point)

## IMPLEMENTATION
- Test harness: Rust test binary with configurable seed range
- Parallelizable: each schedule is independent (run on multiple cores)
- CI: run all 500 schedules on every PR (target: < 10 minutes total)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] 500 schedules generated and executed
- [ ] 100% pass rate (zero consistency violations after recovery)
- [ ] All fsync'd data preserved after crash
- [ ] Failure reproducers saved for any failing schedule
- [ ] Total runtime < 10 minutes on CI hardware
- [ ] Every schedule deterministic (same seed = same result)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:52.676947222Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:19.482390862Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crash","deterministic","replay"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:22.037666789Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.5","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:52.676947222Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":126,"issue_id":"bd-32yn.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-32yn.6","title":"Conformance: fsx-style stress test with corruption injection","description":"# Conformance: fsx-style stress test with corruption injection

## GOAL
Implement an fsx-style (File System eXerciser) stress test that performs random file operations while continuously verifying data integrity. Extended with FrankenFS-specific corruption injection to test self-healing under stress.

## BACKGROUND
fsx (from LTP/xfstests) is the gold standard for filesystem stress testing. It performs random reads, writes, truncates, and mmaps on a file, maintaining an in-memory reference copy. After each operation, it verifies the file matches the reference. Any mismatch is a bug.

## DESIGN

### Operations (weighted random selection)
- write(offset, length, data): 40%
- read(offset, length) + verify: 25%
- truncate(length): 10%
- fsync(): 10%
- fallocate(offset, length): 5%
- punch_hole(offset, length): 5%
- close + reopen: 5%

### Verification
- In-memory reference buffer tracks expected file content
- After each read: compare against reference
- Periodically: close file, reopen, read entire file, compare against reference
- After unmount + remount: verify entire file against reference

### Corruption Injection Extension
- Every N operations (configurable, default 1000): inject random block corruption
- Wait for scrub daemon to detect and repair
- Verify file still matches reference after repair
- Evidence ledger check: repair logged correctly

### Parameters
- File size: configurable (default 64MB)
- Operation count: configurable (default 100000)
- Seed: deterministic
